content.text: Realtime APIs let a voice agent speak and listen over a single persistent connection with sub-300 ms latency — the model streams audio out while simultaneously accepting audio in, enabling the fluid back-and-forth of natural conversation. This section covers the OpenAI Realtime API, the WebSocket and WebRTC transports it supports, the event-driven message protocol, and how to integrate it through LiveKit's Agents framework.

) is a stateful WebSocket (or WebRTC) endpoint that accepts raw audio input and streams audio output continuously in a single session. Unlike the standard Chat Completions API — which is request/response over HTTPS — the Realtime API keeps a persistent bidirectional channel open so that audio can flow in both directions simultaneously.
(a list of turns, just like the messages array in Chat Completions) and an
where incoming microphone audio accumulates until a turn boundary is detected. The model can respond with audio, text, or both (called
) and can call registered functions mid-response, allowing the agent to perform actions (look up a database, call an external service) and speak the result back to the user.
— server-side (Node.js, Python). Audio is base64-encoded inside JSON messages. Lower infrastructure overhead; the application server holds the API key.

— browser-side. The browser negotiates a direct media connection to OpenAI using the Web Audio API and the browser's native WebRTC stack. Audio travels as a proper RTP stream rather than base64 JSON, cutting about 30–50 ms from end-to-end latency. Requires an ephemeral token from your server.

Before Realtime APIs existed, building a voice agent required chaining three separate services: a Speech-to-Text model, a language model, and a Text-to-Speech model. Each hop added latency (typically 400–800 ms per stage) and threw away the prosodic nuance captured in the raw audio — the hesitations, emphasis, and emotional tone that carry meaning a transcript alone cannot preserve.
The Realtime API collapses the pipeline:

is natively multimodal, accepting audio tokens directly. The model generates audio tokens and text tokens in parallel, so the transcript and the spoken response emerge together. The result is round-trip latencies in the 200–400 ms range on a typical connection, close enough to human conversational norms that users experience it as natural dialogue rather than a transcription-then-response delay.

This matters most for interactive voice agents: phone IVR replacement, voice-first customer support, real-time tutoring, and any scenario where the 1–3 s latency of a chained pipeline breaks the conversational rhythm.
A single model processes audio input and emits audio output; no STT→LLM→TTS pipeline overhead.
The model hears tone, pace, and emotion — information a transcript discards. It can adjust response style accordingly.
Output audio starts arriving before the model finishes generating it, so playback begins early.
Server-side VAD (Voice Activity Detection) automatically identifies when the user has stopped speaking and commits the buffer, eliminating the need to ship a separate VAD model.

Tools registered in the session config work exactly like Chat Completions function calling: the model emits a function call event, the client executes it, and returns the result to continue the response.
When the user speaks while the agent is talking, the server detects speech, cancels the in-flight response, and starts a new turn — enabling natural overlapping conversation.
PCM16 at 24 kHz for high quality; G.711 μ-law/a-law at 8 kHz for telephony stack compatibility.

Audio tokens are priced separately from text tokens (roughly $0.06/min input, $0.24/min output at current rates). For tasks that do not require low latency, a cheaper chained STT+LLM+TTS approach may be preferable.
The conversation lives in the open WebSocket connection. If the connection drops, the session is lost — you must implement reconnect logic and optionally replay recent context.
Long conversations consume context tokens. Very long sessions require truncating old turns to stay within the limit.
The Realtime API does not store conversation history across sessions. Your application must serialize and replay context if continuity across calls is needed.
Encoding PCM16 audio as base64 increases payload size by ~33%. WebRTC avoids this but adds NAT traversal complexity and is browser-only.
supports the Realtime API. You cannot swap in a different model without leaving the protocol.

field that identifies the event. The flow for a typical voice turn looks like this:

mode, the server automatically detects silence and commits the buffer, triggering a response. In

mode the server analyzes every chunk for voice activity and automatically commits when silence exceeds the configured

event when the user begins speaking, which the client can use to cancel any in-flight response (barge-in).

attrs.alt: OpenAI Realtime API WebSocket event flow diagram

content.text: The diagram shows the two-sided event flow across a single WebSocket connection. Client-to-server events (amber) carry audio buffer data, session configuration, and manual triggers. Server-to-client events (purple) deliver streamed audio deltas, text transcript deltas, function call arguments, and completion signals. The footer shows the two supported audio formats and the ephemeral token mechanism for browser-side connections.
Reach for the Realtime API when
conversational latency is the primary constraint
. The sweet spot is interactive dialogue where a 1–2 s round-trip breaks the conversational flow:
replacing phone IVR trees with natural language.
where the agent needs to respond immediately to a student's spoken answer.
for users who rely primarily on voice.
where the audio format must be G.711 and round-trip latency is tightly constrained by the carrier network.

latency-sensitive — batch transcription, async summarization, long-form voice memos — the chained STT+LLM+TTS pipeline is cheaper and gives you more model flexibility. Use the Realtime API specifically when the user is waiting in real time for an answer.

Server-side connections hold the API key on the backend and proxy audio between the WebSocket and the user's microphone/speaker. This is the most common production pattern: the browser connects to your server over a normal WebSocket or WebRTC session, and your server connects to OpenAI.
Open the WebSocket with the correct headers (see Examples).
to configure voice, audio format, VAD, and tools.
Capture microphone audio in the correct format (PCM16, 24 kHz, mono) and send
chunks and write them to an audio output stream as they arrive.
, execute the tool and return results with
For browser-direct connections, the API key must never be exposed in client code. Instead, the browser requests a short-lived

from your server (which holds the real key). The browser uses this token to open a WebRTC connection directly to OpenAI. Ephemeral tokens expire after 60 seconds — they are only used to bootstrap the connection, not kept for the session's lifetime.
Create the ephemeral token server-side with a
— pass this to the browser. The browser performs the WebRTC SDP offer/answer exchange with
using the ephemeral token in the
JavaScript — WebSocket connection and event handling
JavaScript — Function calling in a Realtime session

import asyncio
import base64
import json
import os

import websockets

REALTIME_URL = (
    "wss://api.openai.com/v1/realtime"
    "?model=gpt-4o-realtime-preview-2024-12-17"
)
HEADERS = {
    "Authorization": f"Bearer {os.environ['OPENAI_API_KEY']}",
    "OpenAI-Beta": "realtime=v1",
}


async def realtime_session() -> None:
    async with websockets.connect(REALTIME_URL, extra_headers=HEADERS) as ws:
        # 1. Wait for session.created
        msg = json.loads(await ws.recv())
        assert msg["type"] == "session.created"
        print(f"Session: {msg['session']['id']}")

        # 2. Configure the session
        await ws.send(json.dumps({
            "type": "session.update",
            "session": {
                "modalities": ["audio", "text"],
                "voice": "alloy",
                "input_audio_format": "pcm16",
                "output_audio_format": "pcm16",
                "turn_detection": {
                    "type": "server_vad",
                    "threshold": 0.5,
                    "silence_duration_ms": 500,
                },
            },
        }))

        # 3. Inject a text message to trigger a response (demo: no mic needed)
# ... (truncated for brevity)
JavaScript — Ephemeral token for browser-side WebRTC
provides a production-ready wrapper around the OpenAI Realtime API through its
package. Rather than managing the raw WebSocket protocol, you declare a
and LiveKit handles session management, barge-in, audio routing, and room lifecycle.

# pip install livekit-agents livekit-plugins-openai livekit-plugins-silero

from livekit.agents import AutoSubscribe, JobContext, WorkerOptions, cli, llm
from livekit.agents.voice_assistant import VoiceAssistant
from livekit.plugins import openai, silero


async def entrypoint(ctx: JobContext) -> None:
    await ctx.connect(auto_subscribe=AutoSubscribe.AUDIO_ONLY)

    system_prompt = llm.ChatContext().append(
        role="system",
        text=(
            "You are a friendly customer support agent for Acme Corp. "
            "Keep answers brief and clear. If you don't know an answer, say so."
        ),
    )

    assistant = VoiceAssistant(
        vad=silero.VAD.load(),                   # local VAD for barge-in detection
        stt=openai.STT(),                        # Whisper for transcript fallback
        llm=openai.realtime.RealtimeModel(
            model="gpt-4o-realtime-preview",
            voice="alloy",
            temperature=0.7,
# ... (truncated for brevity)
LiveKit handles room-level concerns (multiple participants, recording, PSTN dial-in) while the

plugin keeps the underlying WebSocket to OpenAI open and routes audio between the LiveKit room and the Realtime API. This is the most practical path to a production voice agent that needs scalable room management.
Sending audio in the wrong format

The API expects PCM16 (signed 16-bit little-endian) at 24 kHz, 1 channel. Sending MP3, WAV with a header, or Float32 will silently produce garbled output or no response at all. Always strip container headers and resample before encoding to base64.
fires while the model is speaking, you must send

stop playing the current audio output immediately. If you continue playing stale audio, the user hears the model finishing its sentence while also hearing the new response begin — a confusing double-voice situation.
Forgetting to send response.create after a function result
item, the model waits — it does not automatically continue. You must send
to trigger the next response turn. Omitting this leaves the session silent indefinitely.
Exposing the API key in browser code

The API key grants full account access. Never bundle it in a browser app. Use the ephemeral token flow: your server creates a short-lived token, the browser uses it once to establish the WebRTC connection, and the token expires in 60 seconds regardless.
Sending audio chunks that are too large

Large base64 payloads (> 1 second of audio per message) increase WebSocket message latency. Keep chunks to 100–200 ms of audio (~3,200–6,400 bytes of PCM16 before base64 encoding) so the API can begin processing immediately rather than waiting for a large buffer to arrive.

) gives you precise control but requires you to ship and tune your own VAD. The server-side VAD is accurate and already tuned for the model's latency targets; prefer it unless you have a specific reason to override.
Play audio deltas as they arrive.
events and play at the end — that defeats the purpose of streaming. Pipe each chunk into your audio output pipeline (Web Audio API, PortAudio, ALSA) as it arrives.
When you cancel a response on barge-in, record the
of the cancelled turn so you can send
to keep the conversation state consistent with what the user actually heard.

WebSocket connections drop — network timeouts, server-side session limits, transient errors. Implement a reconnect loop that replays the system prompt and any context turns from your local copy before re-starting the audio stream.
in session config causes the server to emit
events with the transcript of what the model heard. This is invaluable during development for diagnosing why a response veered off-topic.
Realtime tool calls occur mid-conversation; a poorly described tool or ambiguous parameter names cause the model to hallucinate calls with wrong arguments. Write precise

A typical Realtime API round trip from end-of-user-speech to first audio output chunk is 200–400 ms: ~50 ms network RTT, ~100–150 ms for the model to begin generating, and the first audio delta arriving shortly after. WebRTC saves roughly 30–50 ms over WebSocket because audio does not traverse a base64 JSON serialization path.

Each open WebSocket connection is an independent session. There is no shared state between sessions, so horizontal scaling is straightforward: run multiple worker processes, each handling one or more connections. LiveKit's Agent framework handles worker assignment automatically when multiple agent workers register against the same LiveKit server.

Audio tokens are more expensive than text tokens. For high-volume applications, audit whether every session needs native audio output or whether a cheaper STT+text LLM+TTS chain suffices for some query types. A hybrid architecture routes latency-sensitive turns to the Realtime API and batch tasks to cheaper endpoints.

Very long sessions (> 30 minutes) accumulate conversation history that approaches the context window limit. Implement periodic summarization: collapse older turns into a system-level summary message and remove the raw turns to free context tokens.
Production voice agent framework with room management, barge-in, and Realtime plugin
Low-level WebSocket client for Node.js; used for raw Realtime API connections
Async WebSocket client for Python; used in low-level Realtime sessions
Capture and play PCM16 audio on the local machine during development and testing
Browser-native API for capturing mic audio and playing back PCM16 chunks in real time
Resampling and format conversion (e.g. MP3 → PCM16 24 kHz) in server-side pipelines
Ephemeral tokens are mandatory for browser clients.

The Realtime API key grants full account access including billing. Any JavaScript that runs in a browser can be inspected — never hard-code or pass the real API key to the client. The ephemeral token flow from
issues a short-lived credential (60 s TTL) that can only bootstrap a single WebRTC session. Even if intercepted, it expires before it can be reused.
for WebSocket). Audio data is encrypted in transit. There is no unencrypted
OpenAI imposes concurrent session limits per organization tier. For high-concurrency deployments, monitor the
response headers and implement a connection pool with a queue so that bursts do not result in dropped connections.

Even though the function call arguments come from the model, treat them as untrusted input. Validate all arguments against a schema before executing any side-effecting logic (database writes, external API calls). The model can be manipulated by adversarial user prompts (prompt injection through audio) to produce unexpected tool arguments.
If the WebSocket drops mid-conversation, the user should hear a clear error message ("I'm sorry, I lost the connection — let me reconnect") rather than silence. Implement a heartbeat ping (WebSocket
frames) to detect dead connections before they time out naturally, and reconnect with exponential back-off capped at 8–16 seconds.
is a stateful, event-driven WebSocket (or WebRTC) protocol that accepts audio in and streams audio out, enabling sub-400 ms voice round trips.
; WebRTC is the browser-native transport with ~50 ms lower latency. Ephemeral tokens (60 s TTL) are required for browser-direct connections to protect the API key.
: PCM16 at 24 kHz, 16-bit, mono is the high-quality default; G.711 μ-law/a-law at 8 kHz is the telephony-compatible option. Both are base64-encoded in WebSocket mode.
automatically commits the audio buffer on silence; in
events; after executing the tool, return results with
is the production-recommended path: it manages session lifecycle, barge-in, audio routing, and room scaling on top of the raw Realtime API protocol.
The Realtime API is more expensive than chained pipelines
— use it when sub-500 ms latency is required; fall back to a STT+LLM+TTS chain for batch or non-interactive workloads.
